serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw swordfish is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium fillet portion before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Swordfish, raw — NDB No. 15121 / FDC ID 175159
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy; cross-referenced with Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macronutrient values (energy 144 kcal, protein 19.66 g, fat 6.65 g, carbohydrate 0 g, sodium 81 mg, potassium 418 mg, calcium 5 mg, iron 0.38 mg, cholesterol 66 mg) are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (approved primary regional source, confidence 0.95). These values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 (swordfish, raw), which is an exact species match and an approved secondary global source. Saturated fat (1.82 g), monounsaturated fat (2.47 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.62 g), trans fat (0.0 g), niacin (10.0 mg), vitamin B6 (0.39 mg), vitamin B12 (1.66 mcg), phosphorus (286 mg), magnesium (29 mg), zinc (1.14 mg), selenium (52.5 mcg), and water (73.0 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 to supplement HPB values where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed in raw swordfish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — not applicable; basis: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 confirming 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g
gi_source.source_match_notes: Swordfish is a high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate fish with 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175159) and HPB Singapore Foodo. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned per Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to negligible-carbohydrate animal protein foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (81 mg/100 g) and high potassium (418 mg/100 g) with a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Raw swordfish is suitable for hypertensive individuals due to its low sodium content and high potassium, which supports vascular tone; preparation should avoid added salt or high-sodium marinades, and mercury content warrants limiting consumption to 1–2 servings per week.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; high protein (19.66 g/100 g) slows gastric emptying without triggering postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Swordfish is an excellent protein source for individuals with type 2 diabetes as it contains no carbohydrates or sugar, poses no glycaemic load, and its high protein content supports satiety and glycaemic stability; avoid high-sugar glazes or marinades and limit to 1–2 servings per week due to mercury content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) from polyunsaturated fat (1.62 g/100 g) support endothelial function and reduce triglycerides; saturated fat is moderate at 1.82 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Swordfish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids that reduce triglycerides and support endothelial function, making it suitable for cardiovascular disease management when prepared without added saturated fats; however, its elevated methylmercury content means consumption should be limited to 1–2 servings per week, and individuals on anticoagulant therapy should consult their clinician.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Swordfish (Xiphias gladius), raw, refers to fresh uncooked boneless fillets of swordfish, a large, highly migratory pelagic fish found in temperate and tropical oceans worldwide. The flesh is firm, pinkish-white to ivory in colour, meaty and dense in texture, with a mild, slightly sweet flavour that becomes more pronounced when cooked. Swordfish is commonly prepared by grilling, baking, pan-searing, or broiling, and is popular in Mediterranean, American, and global seafood cuisines. Per 100 g raw, swordfish provides 144 kcal, 19.66 g protein, 6.65 g total fat (including 1.82 g saturated fat and approximately 1.62 g polyunsaturated fat including EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids), and 0 g carbohydrates. It is rich in potassium (418 mg), selenium (52.5 mcg), niacin (10 mg), vitamin B12 (1.66 mcg), phosphorus (286 mg), and zinc (1.14 mg). Sodium is low at 81 mg per 100 g. Clinically, swordfish is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes (zero carbohydrate load), hypertension (low sodium, high potassium), and cardiovascular disease (omega-3 fatty acids). A key caution is its elevated methylmercury content as a large predatory fish; health authorities including the US FDA and Singapore HPB recommend limiting swordfish to 1–2 servings per week, particularly for pregnant women, nursing mothers, and young children. Glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content.

What are the omega-3 benefits of swordfish?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (approved primary regional source) with 0.95 confidence, consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 (exact species match, approved secondary global source). Both are approved sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as swordfish is a pure animal protein and fat source containing no carbohydrates. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same basis. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 for raw swordfish. Saturated_fat_g (1.82 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.47 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.62 g), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (10.0 mg), vitamin_b6_mg (0.39 mg), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.66 mcg), phosphorus_mg (286 mg), magnesium_mg (29 mg), zinc_mg (1.14 mg), selenium_mcg (52.5 mcg), and water_g (73.0 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 (swordfish, raw) to supplement HPB values. Fields set to -1 (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg): these micronutrients are not reported or are reported as negligible/trace in both HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 for raw swordfish and cannot be confirmed from approved sources as of 2026-04-16. Swordfish is a large predatory fish with elevated methylmercury; consumption should be limited to 1–2 servings per week per Singapore HPB and US FDA guidance. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g per 100 g confirmed by HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy). The input record's rheumatoid_arthritis health_suitability entry contained an internal contradiction (marked suitable: false but cited omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefits as a positive factor); this condition is not mandatory under VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md and has been omitted from the output.
